So as you go through, when you guys do have a chance to read the expert secrets book, you'll be able to see each of these is kind of sketched out. And so we kind of broke it down in here. And initially, I was trying to do one slide for each thing, but I realized that for me, typically, as I build a slide, a lot of times, sometimes there's one slide, sometimes there's like five or six slides that kind of go through it. So for the title slide, I'll kind of show you guys some of mine. The easiest thing to do is right here is to plug in the how to without statement that we created earlier. So if you have that, you can plug it in. Or if you want to fancify and make it kind of tweak it around, you can do something kind of like I did. So I have two examples. This is my other one. It's the same message, same. It's just different hook to bring people in. This is the one I've used for most of it because this is our highest converting one. So my weird niche funnel is currently making me blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. So it's just a different headline. But both of these headlines, this one and this one, were both for the same presentation. It's just different kind of hooks that brought it in. Okay, because this is like 90% of it or so, so we should probably talk about it. When you're doing a webinar, there's one thing, when I'm in a stage for people, I can get them reacting really, really easily. When you're on a webinar and you're desk behind a microphone and you don't see anybody, it's really weird and you're going to feel awkward and nervous and it's going to be. What I need to do is I have to get people to say yes over and over and over again. The back store behind that, I learned from Ted Thomas. If you guys Google Ted Thomas like closing, there's videos from YouTube where he'll be like a Robert Kiyosaki event and he'll have a line of people, like 200 people following him with their credit cards in hand and they're all waving it. He's like, come on back and he walks to the back of the room and they call him the Pied Piper of sales because everyone's doing it. I was in an event like this and I was speaking, I was selling, I looked in the back and I saw Ted Thomas. I was like, this has got awkward. I'm pitching and the dude is right there and I did my thing and afterwards he's like, Russell, I want to take you to lunch. I'm like, okay. So we go there and he starts to ask me all these questions and we're going through lunch, we're talking and he's like, what are you doing right now? I'm like, what do you mean? He's like, look at your head. I was like, oh, it's moving around. He's like, you've been nodding since we started talking. I'm like, okay. He's like, when I was in your room, he's like, what happened? I was looking in the back of the room and you were doing a great presentation but everybody was sitting there with their heads like this. The reason why I close so well is I ask people to say yes thousands of times in my presentation. From the second I start my presentation, I'm asking them all these questions. He calls them trial closes to get them to say yes. If you watch when I'm speaking, everybody's heads in the room the entire time. It's like a C of P of heads bobbing the whole time. They do it by asking little yes questions. So when I did that, listen to how many times I got you guys to say yes. Okay. Hey, my name is Russell Brunson. And I started telling the story about, but, uh, I was learning internet marketing and I was on a tele-seminar and this guy was talking about how he was a millionaire. And I was like, oh, how cool that if I could be a millionaire, how cool would that be? But I was like, that's not going to happen. Like I'm a wrestler. I'm not that smart, barely graduating school. I was like, but what if I could do that? Like what would that look like? So we're down the number of million dollars on a pad of paper. And I was like, what's the math on that? So I did some math on my little calculator and I found out the million divided by 365 is $2,739.73 a day. I saw that number. I'm like, well, maybe I could actually do that. Now a couple of things on this one, what most people do to qualify themselves is super annoying. Do not be the super annoying person. For example, one time I was interviewing a guy and he was, and, uh, before I interviewed him, he's like, Hey, I'm going to send you my bio so you can, um, read it before you introduce me and I'm like, okay, so he sends me a bio. It was an eight page bio with a mind map, a mind map. He built the mind map for his bio. I was like, I'm reading this thing. I was like, dude, like, so annoying. Okay. The most annoying thing you can do is like brag about how cool you are. So you have to brag about yourself in a way that's like, like non-salon. We're just kind of like, you're doing it, but like, you don't look like a jerk. Cause like then I don't know, I hate when people do that way. So for me, like I like coming off as kind of like the Oshuk's like person. I think that's so much more authentic than like, let me read my bio really quick. I've been working for a blah, blah, blah, blah. Like it's all this techno babble that's important to you, but not to them. Right. So for mine, what I do is I try to think like, what are some things that I've done in my past that are cool. I can share. So I'm like, yeah, like this one time I had this cool chance to speak at Tony Robbins business mastery and feed you, which was like the most insane experience ever. And then he had my whole family come and this is the picture we got on top of his house, um, uh, overlooking like the ocean. It was so insanely cool. In fact, a little while ago, I had a chance to build a funnel with him and we built his book funnel and like, oh, it's the coolest thing in the world. So I'm sharing this cool thing that like, I'm associated myself with something bigger. I'm not like me and Tony Robbins are best friends. He texts me every once in a while. Like that's, you know, it's more like, dude, this is like the coolest thing to happen to me. Like I don't want to brag about like, this is cool. I'm trying to like let them know.
